text: The robotic cholecystectomy begins with the gallbladder visible against the brown liver. The liver turns green using ICG dye and Firefly mode on the DaVinci XI system. The surgeon employs a dome down technique, creating a window to lift the gallbladder. The gallbladder is swollen due to acute cholecystitis, with edema and fluid visible between it and the liver. The gallbladder is closely attached to the liver, and the video is sped up to show the dissection process.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel in the right hand for dissection and a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand for electrocautery, while a third instrument retracts the liver upward. The gallbladder is dissected off the liver, revealing some bleeding and scar tissue indicative of acute on chronic cholecystitis.

In Firefly mode, the liver appears bright green while the gallbladder remains gray due to acute cholecystitis. The surgeon continues dissecting the gallbladder from the liver, moving closer to the neck where the ducts are located.

The surgeon uses bipolar electrocautery to achieve hemostasis, addressing minor bleeding. The gallbladder is being freed from the liver, which is retracted upwards. Fatty tissue appears yellow, while the common bile duct is visible as a bright green structure, indicating areas to avoid during dissection. The gallbladder shows minimal green dye at its neck, suggesting limited dye entry. The surgeon continues to free the gallbladder, observing green dye within the gallbladder and cystic duct under the instrument.

The surgeon continues to free the cystic duct, revealing a clear Y configuration where the cystic duct joins the common bile duct. The gallbladder is visible to the left of the instrument, surrounded by fatty tissue. Using Firefly mode, the anatomy is distinctly visible, with the common bile duct seen extending upwards into the liver and downwards towards the intestines.

The surgeon observes green bile in the duodenum and antrum, indicating bile flow into the intestines. A clip applier is used to place two permanent clips on the cystic duct, followed by dividing the duct with the same instrument. The anatomy is clearly visible throughout the procedure.
